title: Conditionals
subtitle: Teach your programs to make decisions, just like you do.
goal: Use booleans, comparison and logical operators, if / else if / else, nesting, and the ternary operator to make a program choose what to do.

blocks.heading: Programs that decide 🤔
blocks.html:
Until now your programs did the same thing every time. Today they get to make choices: if it's raining, take an umbrella; else wear sunglasses.

To choose, a program asks a yes/no question. In Java a yes/no answer has a special type called boolean, and it can only be true or false.

blocks.heading: Comparison operators
blocks.html:
We ask questions using comparison operators. Each one gives back a boolean:

  • == equal to (careful: two equals signs!)
  • != not equal to
  • < less than, > greater than
  • <= less or equal, >= greater or equal

blocks.caption: A boolean is just true or false
blocks.output:
Score: 82
Passed? true
Is it a perfect 100? false
blocks.explain: score >= 60 is true, so passed is true. score == 100 is false. Notice == compares; a single = would try to assign instead.

blocks.html: One equals vs two! A single = means "put this value into the box." Two == means "are these equal?" Mixing them up is the most common beginner bug.

blocks.heading: if / else if / else
blocks.html:
The if statement runs a block of code only when its question is true. The code to run goes inside curly braces { }.

You can chain else if for more choices, and a final else as the "otherwise" catch-all. Java checks them top to bottom and stops at the first one that's true.

blocks.caption: Turn a score into a letter grade
blocks.explain: score is 82. The first question (>= 90) is false, so Java tries the next: 82 >= 80 is true, so it prints Grade: B and skips the rest.

blocks.question: What values can a boolean hold?
blocks.explain: A boolean is the simplest type in Java: it is either true or false, nothing else.

blocks.heading: Logical operators: && || !
blocks.html:
Sometimes one question isn't enough. Logical operators join booleans together:

  • && AND — true only if both sides are true
  • || OR — true if either side is true
  • ! NOT — flips true to false and back

Also: to compare text, use .equals(), not ==. For example name.equals("Sam").

blocks.caption: even/odd with %, and a can-you-ride check
blocks.explain: number % 2 is the remainder after dividing by 2; if it's 0 the number is even. The ride needs height >= 110 AND a ticket — && is true only when both are true.

blocks.html: Ternary shortcut: condition ? a : b is a tiny if/else that gives back a value: if the condition is true you get a, otherwise b. Great for picking between two values in one line.

blocks.question: When is height >= 110 && hasTicket true?
options: When at least one part is true
Only when both parts are true
blocks.explain: && means AND. Both the height check AND hasTicket must be true for the whole thing to be true.

blocks.question: How should you compare two Strings in Java?
blocks.explain: For text, use name.equals("..."). Using == on Strings can give surprising results, so .equals() is the safe choice.

blocks.title: Teenager check
blocks.instructions: The variable age is 15. Write an if/else that prints "You are a teenager." when age is 13 or more, otherwise prints "Not a teenager yet." Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your program made its first real decision! 🎉
blocks.failMsg: Use an if with the condition age >= 13, and an else for the other case.

blocks.title: Password checker
blocks.instructions: Change guess so it matches password, then use guess.equals(password) in an if to print "Access granted! ✅". If it doesn't match, print "Wrong password. ❌".
blocks.passMsg: Correct — that's exactly how Java compares text. 🔐
blocks.failMsg: Use guess.equals(password) inside the if, and make sure guess matches password.

challenge.title: Weather outfit picker
challenge.instructions: The variable temperature is 30. Use if / else if / else to print clothing advice: 30 or hotter prints a hot-weather message, 15 or warmer (but under 30) prints a mild message, and anything colder prints a cold message. Try changing temperature and re-running!
challenge.hint: Check the hottest case first with if (temperature >= 30), then else if (temperature >= 15), then a plain else for the cold case.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Brilliant! You taught a program to dress for the weather. Day 6 complete — you can make decisions now!
